era_context: Middle Bronze Age II. The Old Babylonian Empire was in terminal decline. The Hittite Old Kingdom was expanding and consolidating. Egypt was fragmented in the Second Intermediate Period. The Indus Valley civilization was in its final phases. Mycenaean Greece was in the Early Bronze Age III. This was a period of significant political reorganization across the Near East and Eastern Mediterranean.

events.title: Old Babylonian administrative and military decline
events.description: The Old Babylonian state continued to lose territorial control and administrative coherence. Samsu-iluna's reign (c. 1749–1712 BCE) was marked by military setbacks and the loss of outlying territories. The kingdom was increasingly confined to the Babylon region proper. This decline was part of a broader pattern of state collapse in the early 2nd millennium BCE, possibly linked to climate stress (aridification) and population movements.

sources.name: Babylonian king lists and administrative texts

events.certainty_note: The decline is attested in cuneiform sources, though specific events in -1703 are not recorded.

disconfirming_evidence: No specific disconfirming evidence identified for events listed. The broader chronological framework for the Old Babylonian period remains contested.
historiographic_note: Documentation for -1703 is essentially non-existent. No contemporary records document this specific year. The historical narrative is inferred from broader patterns in king lists and administrative texts. The record is heavily skewed toward Mesopotamia; other regions are invisible in written sources. Archaeological evidence provides context but cannot pinpoint events to specific years. This year is largely a placeholder in the historical record.

graph_edges.note: Continuation of the broader fragmentation process documented in -1702.
